coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave fabrication of highly luminescent nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots for the determination of glutathione in pharmaceutical supplements
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kamal, Amira H.
Kannouma, Reham E.
Hammad, Mohamed A.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: A facile and sensitive nano-probe for spectrofluorometric glutathione (GSH) determination has been developed based on dual-doped nitrogen and phosphorus carbon quantum dots (NP@CQDs). The NP@CQDs were prepared, through a rapid, one-step microwave heating method in only 180 s with a high quantum yield (0.63) using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid as carbon and nitrogen precursor and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as nitrogen and phosphorus dopant. The synthesised NP@CQDs had a maximum bluish fluorescence emission at 420 nm, after excitation at 360 nm. The luminescence of the prepared NP@CQDs was quenched via a static quenching mechanism by ferrous ions, while ferric ions did not affect the emission of NP@CQDs. GSH was mixed with ferric ions before adding NP@CQDs to exploit this observation. The attenuation in emission observed after adding NP@CQDs to GSH/ferric ions was directly related to the GSH concentration, as GSH worked to reduce the ferric ions present and produce ferrous ions. The proposed fluorometric method was validated in compliance with the International Council on Harmonization (ICH) guidelines. The proposed fluorescent nano-probe showed good linearity for GSH determination over a range of 0.5–10 µg/mL with % recoveries ranging between 99.50 and 101.86 %, and RSD less than 2 %. NP@CQDs were implemented for GSH determination in pharmaceutical supplementary capsules with respectable accuracy and precision. This approach is simple, reliable, accurate, specific, and it also does not require expensive chemicals or sophisticated equipment. This work opens up other uses of NP@CQDs in pharmaceutical and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione
Microwave synthesis
Nitrogen doping
Phosphorus doping
Carbon quantum dots
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reaction of ferric ions and GSH
Reaction of NP@CQDs and the quencher

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Field emission-TEM image of the NP@CQDs (inset: lattice spacing of 0.251 nm,) (B) Histogram of particle size distribution, (C) FT-IR spectrum of the NP@CQDs, and (D) XPS survey spectrum of the NP@CQDs.
Effect of Fe3+ (40 µM), GSH (10 µg/mL), mixture of both (Fe3+/GSH) on the fluorescence of NP@CQDs (inset: effect of Fe2+ on the fluorescence of NP@CQDs).
Stern–Volmer plots between F°/F and concentration of GSH (µM) at different temperatures.

(A) Response of NP@CQDs towards GSH (10 µg/mL), Fe3+(40 µM), (Fe3+/GSH) and different inorganic metals 20 µg/mL (B) Response of Fe3+/GSH/NP@CQDs system towards possible interferants (GSH:10 µg/mL, Fe3+:40 µM, Glycine, N-acetyl cysteine: 30 µg/mL, other interferants: 1000 µg/mL).

The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) of (A) the developed spectrofluorimetric method and (B) the reported HPLC method. The analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE) of (C) the developed spectrofluorimetric method and (D) the reported HPLC method. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Analytical performance data for determination of GSH by the developed approach.
Intraday and interday precision data for the determination of GSH by the developed approach.
Statistical performance of the developed approach and reported HPLC one for the determination of GSH in Glutathione® capsules.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Glutathione® capsule is labelled to contain 250 mg of GSH.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The tabulated values of F and t-test at p = 0.05.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave fabrication of highly luminescent nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots for the determination of glutathione in pharmaceutical supplements

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Sadat City, Monufia 32897, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Sadat City, Monufia 32897, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: at: Elgeish Street, The Medical Campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Medical Campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and sensitive nano-probe for spectrofluorometric glutathione (GSH) determination has been developed based on dual-doped nitrogen and phosphorus carbon quantum dots (NP@CQDs). The NP@CQDs were prepared, through a rapid, one-step microwave heating method in only 180 s with a high quantum yield (0.63) using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid as carbon and nitrogen precursor and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as nitrogen and phosphorus dopant. The synthesised NP@CQDs had a maximum bluish fluorescence emission at 420 nm, after excitation at 360 nm. The luminescence of the prepared NP@CQDs was quenched via a static quenching mechanism by ferrous ions, while ferric ions did not affect the emission of NP@CQDs. GSH was mixed with ferric ions before adding NP@CQDs to exploit this observation. The attenuation in emission observed after adding NP@CQDs to GSH/ferric ions was directly related to the GSH concentration, as GSH worked to reduce the ferric ions present and produce ferrous ions. The proposed fluorometric method was validated in compliance with the International Council on Harmonization (ICH) guidelines. The proposed fluorescent nano-probe showed good linearity for GSH determination over a range of 0.5–10 µg/mL with % recoveries ranging between 99.50 and 101.86 %, and RSD less than 2 %. NP@CQDs were implemented for GSH determination in pharmaceutical supplementary capsules with respectable accuracy and precision. This approach is simple, reliable, accurate, specific, and it also does not require expensive chemicals or sophisticated equipment. This work opens up other uses of NP@CQDs in pharmaceutical and environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), a tripeptide composed of three amino acids, is a key cellular molecule in many organisms. GSH is the primary thiol group generated chemically by animals, plants, and microbes. This biological molecule serves as a potent antioxidant and plays a critical role in cellular defence mechanisms. Due to its functions in the human body, GSH is thought to play a part in the treatment of several illnesses, such as diabetes, cystic fibrosis, HIV, AIDS, influenza, and certain types of cancer. In common pharmaceutical supplements, GSH is utilized for its diverse therapeutic properties, including detoxification, immune support, and anti-ageing effects [1,2].

Determination of GSH in dosage forms is challenging due to the lack of chromophoric or fluorophoric groups [3]. To enable the spectrofluorometric detection of GSH, nanoparticles have been prepared and applied including carbon nanomaterials, functionalized Au nanoparticles, and rhodamine derivatives [4–7]. Numerous benefits of the spectrofluorometric approach include its ease of use, affordability, high sensitivity, selectivity, and ease of handling samples [8–10]. Because of these advantages, spectrofluorometry was a wise choice for determining GSH.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), one of these fluorescent nanomaterials, have gained increasing prominence recently as fluorescent probes. CQDs that are smaller, less than 10 nm, have drawn special interest because of their superior optical characteristics, ease of synthesis, good water solubility, consistent chemical properties, and excellent biocompatibility [11]. Despite CQDs' many distinctive qualities, their low quantum yields (QY) end up being a disadvantage [12]. Lately, doping heteroatoms like boron (B), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and phosphorous (P) to CQDs has been shown to significantly raise the QY of CQDs [13–17]. The complementary effects of different heteroatoms can produce unique electronic configurations in heteroatom co-doping. N doping results in new electronic configurations and a greater fluorescence QY because N and C atoms have similar atomic radii [18–21]. The P atom's radius is greater than that of the C atom, which means it can replace defects and operate as an n-type donor, changing the CQDs' electrochemical properties. Therefore, co-doping with N or P atoms can improve the quantum yield (QY) and photoelectric properties of the CQDs [22,23]. A variety of techniques, including chemical oxidation, hydro/solvothermal treatment, electrochemical carbonization, and laser ablation, can be used to synthesize CQDs. These preparatory methods' drawbacks are thought to be their extreme conditions and time demands. Conversely, the microwave synthesis method provides homogeneous particle size, a rapid reaction time, minimal energy usage, and simplicity [24].

In the current work, NP@CQDs were synthesized through a rapid and facile microwave heating method in 180 s, using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid di sodium salt (EDTA) as carbon and nitrogen precursor and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as nitrogen and phosphorus dopant to form highly quantum yield (QY) NP@CQDs. Field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), raman spectroscopy, infrared spectroscopy (IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and UV/Vis absorption spectrometry were then used to analyze the NP@CQDs. The reaction between ferric ions and GSH produces ferrous ions that can quench the native fluorescence of NP@CQDs. Because of this, the as-prepared NP@CQDs were used as a fluorescence nanoprobe to determine GSH in pharmaceutical supplements. Compared with the other nanomaterial-based spectrofluorometric methods for GSH determination, this method is more rapid and more sensitive and the prepared NP@CQDs have markedly higher quantum yield.

Glutathione (GSH) (99.90 %) was purchased from Oxford (Maharashtra, India). Phosphoric acid, Na2HPO4, and K2HPO4 were bought from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The disodium salt of EDTA, Na2S, (NH4)2HPO4, FeCl3, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC), magnesium stearate, silicon dioxide, and starch were purchased from Piochem (6th of October, Egypt). Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared using a mixture of 0.05 M acetic acid, 0.05 M phosphoric acid, and 0.05 M boric acid, and the pH was adjusted to 2 using 1 M sodium hydroxide. Teorell and Stenhagen buffer was prepared by mixing 0.05 M disodium hydrogen phosphate, 0.05 M citric acid, 0.05 M boric acid and 1 M sodium hydroxide and pH was adjusted to 2 using 1 M hydrochloric acid. Legionella buffer was prepared by mixing 0.37 g of potassium chloride and 0.038 g of hydrochloric acid and completing the volume to 100 mL with deionized water. Aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water. Glutathione® capsules (NOW FOODS Co., IL-USA). Each capsule is labelled to contain 250 mg GSH.

In 20 mL of deionized water, 0.7 g EDTA and 1.32 g (NH4)2HPO4 were dissolved before sonication for 5 min. The mixture was heated up for 180 s at 900 W in the microwave oven. Once the dark brown residue had formed, it was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, sonicated, and centrifuged for ten minutes at 5000 rpm. Filtration of the supernatant was done using a 0.22 μm membrane. In a 10-mL volumetric flask, 1 mL of stock was added, and the remaining volume was filled with deionized water to create the working solution (10 %v/v). The working solution was stable for two weeks and kept in the refrigerator until needed. After excitation at λex = 360 nm, emission intensities were measured at λem = 420 nm.

A stock solution of FeCl3 (1 mM) was obtained by dissolving 16.2 mg of FeCl3 in 100 mL of deionized water. Ten mg of GSH was put in a 100 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in 100 mL deionized water to obtain a stock solution of 100 µg/mL. Different volumes of GSH stock solutions were added to 400 µL of FeCl3 and incubated for 5 min before the addition of 100 µL of NP@CQDs (10 %v/v) completed the volume to 10 mL with the diluent to obtain GSH concentrations within the range of 0.5–10 μg/mL. Britton-Robinson buffer pH 2 was used as a diluent. After 10 min of incubation, the emission fluorescence intensities were measured at λem = 420 nm after being excited at λex = 360 nm versus the blank. The blank was prepared by mixing 100 µL of NP@CQDs and 400 µL of FeCl3 in a 10 mL volumetric flask and completing the volume with the diluent. To construct the calibration curve, the quenching values (ΔF) were plotted against corresponding GSH concentrations (μg/mL).

Ten Glutathione® capsules (each capsule is labelled to contain 250 mg GSH) were evacuated and weighed accurately. An accurate weight of the powder equivalent to 250 mg of GSH was dissolved in deionized water, sonicated for 5 min, and completed the volume to 250 mL (1 mg/mL). This extract was filtered, and the filtrate was further diluted to 100 µg/mL. To get the final working solutions (2, 4, 7 µg/mL), the corresponding volume was mixed with 400 µL of FeCl3 and incubated for 5 min before the addition of 100 µL of NP@CQDs (10 %v/v) then completed the volume to 10 mL with the diluent. The %recovery values were assessed and compared statistically with those obtained by a reported HPLC method [25].

Spectrophotometric measurements were obtained using a UV/Vis double-beam spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific model Evolution 201, USA). Fluorescence data were collected with a JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) fitted with a 1-cm quartz cell with a slit width of 5 nm at a scanning speed of 1000 nm/min and medium sensitivity. For data processing and acquisition, the Spectra Manager program (Jasco Co., Tokyo, Japan) was utilized. A Thermo Scientific™ Talos F200i (S)TEM field emission (scanning) transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with a 200 kV acceleration voltage was used to examine the morphology and particle size of NP@CQDs. Burker Tensor 27 FT/IR spectrophotometer (Burker, Germany) was used to examine the surface moieties of NP@CQDs. Thermo Scientific™ K-Alpha™ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to acquire NP@CQD X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. TM-25MK Tornado Microwave oven (900 W) was used for the preparation of the NP@CQDs. AXIS balance (ALN220, Gdańsk, Poland) was utilized for weighing. A pH meter (Jenway 3510, Saint Neots, UK) was used for pH measurements. An Elmasonic-S 60H ultrasonic cleaning unit and a Cyan-CL008 bench Centrifuge were used. The SUEZ purification system (Paris, France) was used to deionize water.

The current work describes a spectrofluorometric approach for GSH determination that is easy, affordable, and sensitive. The synthesis process of NP@CQDs resulted in a highly luminous probe that can be utilized to determine GSH.

To obtain NP@CQDs with high quantum yield, several trials were carried out. The choice of EDTA as the carbon and nitrogen source for the CDs' core was made because of its accessibility and affordability in most analytical laboratories. At first, CQDs made solely of EDTA were unable to provide a high fluorescence intensity. The study was therefore focused on doping with heteroatoms to increase the fluorescence intensity. Upon the addition of Na2S, Na2HPO4, or K2HPO4 to EDTA, only a modest increase in the fluorescence intensity occurred. Surprisingly, a noticeable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity was attained upon using (NH4)2HPO4 with EDTA (Fig. S1A). This could be due to the higher number of hydrogen bonding sites of (NH4)2HPO4 which could improve the doping of CQDs aside from being a source of N and P. Table S1 shows the number of hydrogen bond donors and acceptors in each of the tested dopants. These results indicated that using (NH4)2HPO4 during CQDs synthesis can thereby enable P-doping in the CQDs while also boosting the quantity of N already doped from the EDTA itself.

Different molar ratios of EDTA:(NH4)2HPO4 ranging from 1:6 to 4:1, respectively were examined. Mixtures with higher amounts of (NH4)2HPO4 relative to the amount of EDTA resulted in high fluorescence intensities. The increase in the fluorescence emission was related to a corresponding increase in the ratio of (NH4)2HPO4 to EDTA which could be attributed to the increase in the doping of N and P. Accordingly, the mixture of EDTA and (NH4)2HPO4 in a 1:5 M ratio was selected as the optimum as shown in Fig. S1B.

Five levels of microwave power were examined to study the effect of microwave power on the synthesis of the NP@CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S1C, applying low to medium power yielded orange residues that formed weakly fluorescent NP@CQDs. Dark brown residue was produced upon increasing the power to 600 W yielding CDs with relatively high fluorescence. Using the microwave's full power (900 W) resulted in the formation of dark black residue with an apparent increase in NP@CQDs yield. The greatest emission was detected at a microwave power of 900 W, as shown in Fig. S1C. It has been reported that increasing the power of the microwave increases the energy bandgap, the absorption intensity, and hence the emission intensity of the produced NP@CQDs [26]. It was discovered that 180 s was the ideal duration for examining the impact of reaction time in the microwave. Longer microwave times were avoided because the charred, and the container was damaged. Consequently, NP@CQDs using EDTA disodium salt as a precursor and (NH4)2HPO4 as a dopant, in a 1:5 M ratio, using 900 W microwave power for 180 s.

The morphological characterization and optical characteristics of the produced NP@CQDs were investigated utilizing a variety of techniques. Field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) images showed that the synthesized NP@CQDs were homogenous, spherical, and monodispersed with no obvious aggregation with a lattice spacing of 0.251 nm (Fig. 1 A) which corresponds to a gap between the (002) planes [27]. The histogram of particle size distribution showed uniform sizes ranging from 2.5 to 6.0 nm with an average diameter of 3.5–4.0 nm (Fig. 1B). A diffraction peak localized at 23.5° was seen in the NP@CQDs film's X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S2A), which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (002) lattice planes [28]. One of the most effective non-invasive methods for characterizing materials with carbon nanostructures is Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra of the NP@CQDs are displayed in Fig. S2B. Graphitic carbon typically exhibits two common Raman bands, the D-band and the G-band, below 2000 cm−1. The G-band is associated with the vibrations of the sp2-carbon in the graphitic sheets, while the D-band is associated with defects in the graphitic sheets. About 1350 cm−1 was the peak for the D-band, while 1570 cm−1 was related to the G-band [29]. The symbols ID and IG stand for the peak intensities of the D-band and G-band, respectively. IG/ID was used to determine the graphitization of carbon nanostructured materials. The resulting NP@CQDs are highly graphitized, as evidenced by the high-intensity ratio of the G to D band (IG > ID) and an IG/ID value of approximately 1.84 [30]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C) showed a broad peak at 3422 cm−1 which revealed the presence of NH and OH stretching vibrations. The peak in 2096 cm−1 could be assigned to CN stretching, while the sharp peak at 1455 cm−1 reflected the presence of CC and CN. The peaks centred at 1641, 1260, and 1082 cm−1 could be attributed to CO, PO, and POR, respectively [31]. These findings demonstrated the presence of several function moieties such as carboxylic, hydroxyl, amine, and phosphate on the surface of the synthesized CDs promoting their water solubility. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the chemical makeup and chemical bonding of the synthesized NP@CQDs were determined. A survey scan of the NP@CQDs is shown in Fig. 1D, where four notable peaks at eV 134.9(P2p), 287.23(C1s), 401.49(N1s), and 533.1(O1s) were visible. Three peaks at 284.95 (CC/CC), 286.23 (CO), and 288.62 (CO) eV were discernible in the deconvoluted spectra of the C1s signal (Fig. S3A) [32]. Three peaks at 531.27 (CO), 532.63 (COH), and 533.41 (CO) eV were detectable in the deconvoluted spectra of the O1s signal (Fig. S3B) [33]. In the deconvoluted spectra of the N1s signal (Fig. S3C), one peak for CN was found at 400.22 eV whereas the second peak, reflecting the NH group, was found at 401.22 eV [32,34]. The PO and PC/PN groups were represented by the two peaks at 133.46 and 134.4 eV, respectively, in the deconvoluted spectra of the P2p signal (Fig. S3D) [23]. The elemental and chemical bond information obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy has been verified by the results of the XPS examination. The UV/Vis spectrum (Fig. S4) of the prepared NP@CQDs showed an absorption peak at 315 nm which might be attributed to excited state trapping by the surface state, hence increasing emission [35]. It was found that the emission wavelength was controlled by the excitation wavelength. The optimum emission wavelength was selected after manipulating the excitation wavelength over a range of 300–370 nm (Fig. S5). The synthesized NP@CQDs exhibited the maximum fluorescence intensity at 420 nm after excitation at 360 nm.

To calculate the quantum yield, the following formula was applied using the single-point approach [36].
Φ
NP @ C Q D s
=
Φ
st
×
F
NP @ C Q D s
/
F
st
×
η
NP @ C Q D s
/
η
st
×
A
st
/
A
NP @ C Q D s
where Φ NP@CQDs is the quantum yield of the NP@CQDs, Φst is the quantum yield of the standard, FNP@CQDs is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the NP@CQDs, Fst is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the standard, ANP@CQDs is the absorbance value of the NP@CQDs, Ast is the absorbance value of the standard and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The standard fluorescence material was quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φst = 0.54 at 350 nm) and the absorbance values were normalized to 0.05. The NP@CQDs in the current study have a high quantum yield (0.63). This elevated quantum yield could be attributed to the high amount of doping by both nitrogen and phosphorus atoms. Also using a microwave for synthesis provides an incredible fast with little reaction time and uniform distribution of heat for CQDs. It is possible to expect size-controllable nanoparticles with an excellent yield [37].

It was found that GSH, in the presence of ferric ions, had a quenching effect on the native fluorescence of the prepared NP@CQDs. By studying the effect of either GSH, ferric ions and ferrous ions, it was found that the fluorescence intensity was quenched by ferrous ions alone and by the mixture of ferric ions and GSH as observed in Fig. 2 . Neither GSH nor ferric ions alone had a quenching effect on the NP@CQDs. Consequently, it was supposed the ferric ions were reduced by GSH to produce ferrous ions that possessed the quenching effect. Increasing the GSH concentration in the presence of a constant concentration of ferric ions resulted in a quantitative reduction in the NP@CQDs' native fluorescence. Static and dynamic quenching strategies are the two main types of mechanisms for fluorescence quenching. The Stern-Volmer equation was used to demonstrate a probable mechanism responsible for NP@CQDs quenching [38]:
F °
F
= 1 + K s v
Q
where F° and F are the fluorescence emission intensities in the absence and presence of the GSH, respectively; Ksv denotes the Stern–Volmer quenching constant; [Q] denotes the GSH molar concentration. At three distinct temperatures (301, 311, and 321 K), Stern-Volmer plots were investigated as observed in Fig. 3 . It is obvious that increasing the temperature resulted in a decrease in Ksv values, suggesting the possibility of a static quenching mechanism. Also, Fig. S6 showed a new peak at 260 nm in the UV/Vis spectrum of the NP@CQDs after adding ferric and GSH to the NP@CQDs, indicative of the emergence of a non-luminescent ground state complex [39]. According to these observations, the quenching of the native fluorescence of the prepared dots was caused primarily by a static mechanism.

To maximize the sensitivity of the produced nanoprobe, some variables associated with experiments were evaluated and modified. Some of these variables were associated with the reaction of ferric ions with GSH, whereas the others were associated with the reaction of NP@CQDs and the quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction of ferric ions and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The first variable to be optimized was the order of addition. By changing the addition order of GSH, ferric ions and NP@CQDs, the quenching process was affected. Three different orders were tried: NP@CQDs/Fe3+/GSH, NP@CQDs/GSH/Fe3+, Fe3+/GSH/NP@CQDs. The highest quenching was attained by adding ferric ions to GSH first before adding the NP@CQDs (Fig. S7A). This could be explained if NP@CQDs hinders the interaction between Fe3+ and GSH, which might be due to the formation of a Fe3+ stable complex with P atoms in the NP@CQDs. This observation confirmed that the reduction of ferric ions to ferrous ions in the presence of GSH was responsible for the quenching effect on the native fluorescence of NP@CQDs. Also, the concentration of ferric ions can affect the method linearity. There are two requirements for the amount of ferric ions in the reaction; it has to be sufficient to induce substantial quenching and the amount of ferric ions had to be in excess to make GSH the rate-limiting reactant. The quenching increased by increasing the concentration of ferric ions up to 40 µM (Fig. S7B). Thus, the following procedure were performed using 40 µM of ferric ions. Finally, the incubation time of ferric ions and GSH before the addition of NP@CQDs was studied. Fig. S7C showed that five minutes were enough for the reduction of ferric ions by GSH. These results showed that the optimum reaction conditions were attained by adding 40 µM of ferric ions to varying concentrations of GSH for 5 min before adding the NP@CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction of NP@CQDs and the quencher
ce:section.ce:para: After the incubation of 40 µM of ferric ions with GSH for five minutes, NP@CQDs and diluent were added. The diluent pH was optimized using different levels from pH 1 to pH 10 using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH to investigate the influence of pH on fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S8A, the optimal pH was 2, with the most significant quenching effect. Three different universal buffers with pH 2 were tried, Britton-Robinson, Teorell and Sternhagen and Legionella acid. Britton-Robinson and legionella acid buffers produced the maximum quenching effect (Fig. S8B). Britton-Robinson was chosen for further adjustment of the other parameters due to its reliability. Finally, the incubation time between the quencher and NP@CQDs was studied. Incubation times ranging from 1 to 20 min were tried. The outcomes (Fig. S8.C) demonstrated that the reaction was completed within 10 min and no observable change occurred after that. Accordingly, NP@CQDs were added to the pre-mixed Fe3+/GSH and diluted with Britton-Robinson (pH 2), and kept for 10 min before measuring the emission at 420 nm.

Several inorganic metals were used to investigate the specificity of NP@CQDs. Fig. 4 A indicates that GSH in the presence of ferric ions significantly reduces the fluorescence of NP@CQDs when compared to GSH alone, ferric ions, or other mono, di, and trivalent metals (K+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Cr3+). The only interferent metal was ferrous ion as it had the main role in the mechanism of quenching of the NP@CQDs luminescence. Several organic molecules or potential pharmaceutical excipients were examined in terms of specificity. Fig. 4B indicated that the addition of interfering substances does not significantly affect the quenching action of GSH/Fe3+ on the NP@CQDs. The proposed sensor has high specificity and promises to detect GSH in different matrices.

Analyzing the concentration and purity of GSH in pharmaceutical formulations is essential to ensure product quality and efficacy. Accurate GSH analysis in pharmaceutical dosage forms is paramount for maintaining consistency and compliance with regulatory standards. By utilizing validated analytical methods, pharmaceutical manufacturers can ensure that the GSH content in their formulations meets the specified requirements, guaranteeing the intended therapeutic effects for patients. Additionally, monitoring GSH levels in dosage forms enables manufacturers to optimize formulations and storage conditions to preserve the stability and efficacy of the product over time. To evaluate the accuracy and usability of the proposed probe, recovery experiments were carried out to determine GSH content in Glutathione® capsules. The average % recoveries and relative standard deviation were calculated. Table 3 displays % recoveries of 100.70 %–102.70 % (RSD=1.02) for GSH in the pharmaceutical capsules. These satisfactory findings reveal the probe's potential for further applications. At a 95 % confidence level, the proposed method's results were consistent with the reported HPLC/UV approach [25] using Student’s t-test and F-test. The reported method utilized a reversed-phase column (Thermo Hypersil® BDS C18 (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm)) at room temperature with UV detection at 215 nm. The mobile phase was composed of water and acetonitrile (50:50, v/v) running at 1.0 mL/min flow rate (Fig. S9). Although the reported method has proved effective in GSH analysis, but it is expensive, has difficult processes, takes a long time, uses many solvents, and requires skilled staff. This proposed spectrofluorometric approach is simple, fast, inexpensive, and straightforward to use. In addition, the LOD and LOQ of the reported method are 0.6 and 1.8 µg/mL revealing that the developed spectrofluorimetric method is more sensitive than the reported method. Several attempts to determine GSH in various matrices employing luminescent nanoprobes were described (Table S2). The chemiluminescence probe [41] showed complicated preparation steps and was not as sensitive as the proposed probe. Hydrothermal synthesis was widespread [42–44], whereas microwave one [45] was rarely used for these nanoprobes' fabrication. Even microwave-prepared CQDs revealed a poor quantum yield when compared to the high quantum yield NP@CQDs developed in this work.

Although numerous concepts have been proposed for reviewing green procedures in analytical methods, combining green analytical procedure index (GAPI), and analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE) was recommended for a thorough evaluation of a methodology for analysis that produces collaborative outcomes. The developed spectrofluorometric methodology was evaluated using the previously mentioned implements and compared to the reported HPLC method. GAPI offered a complete description of multiple analysis procedures, including sample preparation, handling (collection, preservation, transport, and storage), chemicals used, and instrumentation. Each item was given a different colour, ranging from green to yellow to red, to indicate if it had a low, medium, or high environmental impact, respectively. The developed approach showed a majority of green pictograms and only a few red ones (7 green pictograms and 3 red pictograms). On the other hand, the reported HPLC procedure seemed to be less green (5 green pictograms against 6 red pictograms). Additionally, the AGREE tool was used to depict the greenness pattern as a numerical value. The AGREE score was 0.70 for the spectrofluorometric method and 0.48 for the reported HPLC method, confirming the green perspective of the proposed method (Fig. 5 ).

Remarkably luminescent NP@CQDs were fabricated and employed as a nano-probe for the quantitative fluorometric determination of GSH without the necessity for pre-derivatization. The NP@CQDs were produced in 180 s using inexpensive and easily available precursors and a single-step microwave heating method. The resulting NP@CQDs exhibit a 63.8 % quantum yield. The NP@CQDs that were created displayed a peak blue fluorescence at 420 nm when excited at 360 nm. The prepared NP@CQDs were characterized using several instrumental techniques such as FE-TEM, XRD, raman spectroscopy, FTIR, and XPS. The method depends on the quenching of the fluorescence of NP@CQDs by ferrous ions produced from the reaction between the ferric ions and GSH. It was found that increasing the concentration of GSH had a quantitative static quenching effect on NP@CQDs in the presence of ferric ions by forming a non-luminescent ground state complex. The developed method showed good linearity parameters with a limit of detection of 0.16 μg/mL. The developed method showed satisfactory accuracy, repeatability, and specificity for GSH determination in the dietary supplement capsules. Moreover, the greenness of the developed spectrofluorometric methodology was evaluated by GAPI and AGREE and compared to the reported HPLC method. The developed spectrofluorometric method overweighed the reported HPLC method in greenness assessment.

Amira H. Kamal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Reham E. Kannouma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamed A. Hammad: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Main Chemical Laboratories (MCL) for helping in the characterization of NP@CQDs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111488.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
